other_observers: The bird was seen in the course of a Boulder Bird Club outing with 108 participants. By the time of the sighting, the outing had broken into 4 subgroups of about the same size. Thus, I would say that there were about 25-30 other observers of this bir; Yes, I am the original finder and identifier of this bird. I quickly got the whole group on the bird, and we had about 8 scopes set up on the bird. Nobody else in my "subgroup" was especially adept at identifying gulls. I telephoned two oth
specific_location: Along the edge of Leggett Reservoir. Leggett Reservoir is one of the 3 reservoirs in the Valmont Reservoir complex.
description: I first saw the bird at a distance of about 250-300 feet. It was sitting on the water fairly close to the shore. Without binoculars, I guessed it was going to be a Ring-billed Gull. With binoculars (10X), I was struck by its relatively dark mantle, and I briefly wondered if it was a California Gull. Through a scope (32X), I could easily tell that it was a Mew Gull.  The bird presented itself for leisurely, close-up viewing, and identification as a definitive basic Mew Gull was straightforward. Key marks seen on the sitting bird included: medium-dark mantle with large white "skirt" of folded secondaries; long, pointy black wings, with bold contrasting long, white spot under outermost primary sometimes visible; small-bodied, attenuated shape enhanced by the bird's tendency to ride high on the water and to appear "corky" and buoyant; white underparts; small, gently rounded head with dove-like aspect; large dark eye; slight, almost entirely yellow bill with just a little gray smudging.  After at least 15 minutes of close-up observation (<175 feet through multiple telescopes), the bird flew to the other side of the reservoir, where it was perhaps 750 feet away and not amenable to further observation.  I was not one of the observers who actually saw the bird in flight. However, the bird sometimes thrashed about on the water (see below) permitting additional observation.Behaviors: During most of the period of observation, the bird was simply sitting on the water along the west shore of Leggett Reservoir. On 3 or 4 occasions, however, it thrashed about--probably a bathing behavior. When its wings were upraised, it was easy to see the long white spot on the outermost primary (most evident from beneath).  After 15+ minutes, the bird flew to the other side of the reservoir, and then, eventually, we lost track of it.  Interestingly, it was, at first, the only gull on Leggett Reservoir. During the course of observation, 2 Ring-billed Gulls showed up, but none landed near the Mew Gull. One Ring-billed Gull did, however, fly directly over the Mew Gull (about 40 feet above the water), but the comparison was not especially helpful.Call: The bird was silent during the period of observation.Plumage: definitive basic ("winter adult&
